## Overview

**v0.4.4 — macOS scroll-to-bottom on keystroke.**

A focused patch on top of v0.4.3. It fixes a macOS-only scroll behavior: when
you have scrolled back into the scrollback history and then type, the viewport
now jumps to the bottom (the live prompt) so you can see what you are typing —
matching Windows, Linux, and standard terminal behavior.

This applies to regular typing, IME composition (pre-edit) and commit,
navigation keys (arrows, Home/End), and control combos — including emacs-style
line editing such as Ctrl+A / Ctrl+E, and Ctrl+C. Output alone still does
**not** scroll you down: if you scroll up while a long command keeps printing,
your view stays put (scroll-on-output remains off, as before).

Windows is functionally unchanged from v0.4.3 — it already behaved this way; the
Windows build is bumped to 0.4.4 only for version parity. No config schema
changes, no migration, and no ghostty dependency update.

## Downloads

| Platform | File | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Windows | `tildaz-v0.4.4-win-x64.zip` | Unzip anywhere. Includes `tildaz.exe` + bundled ConPTY runtime. Unsigned — same AV / SmartScreen handling as v0.4.3. Behavior unchanged from v0.4.3. |
| macOS (universal) | `tildaz-v0.4.4-macos.dmg` | Apple Silicon + Intel single binary. Drag `TildaZ.app` into Applications. macOS 13 Ventura or later. |

First-launch on macOS: same ad-hoc-signing setup as v0.4.3 — right-click
`TildaZ.app` → Open, or `xattr -d com.apple.quarantine
/Applications/TildaZ.app`. No re-grant of permissions if you already ran v0.4.3.

## Migration from v0.4.3

None. Same config schema, same defaults.

## What's new since v0.4.3

### macOS scroll-to-bottom on keystroke

- **macOS: typing while scrolled back now jumps to the live prompt**
  ([#242](https://github.com/ensky0/tildaz/issues/242)). Previously, when you
  scrolled up into the scrollback buffer on macOS, only a *committed* character
  reaching the PTY would snap the viewport back to the bottom — so while you
  were mid-composition in an IME (Korean/Japanese/Chinese), or pressing arrow
  keys, the view stayed stranded in history and you could not see what you were
  typing. The macOS host now routes all user key input — regular characters,
  IME pre-edit and commit, navigation keys (arrows, Home/End), and control
  combos such as Ctrl+A / Ctrl+E (emacs-style line editing) and Ctrl+C —
  through the same scroll-to-bottom path that Windows and Linux already used.
  Output-only scrolling is unchanged: long output no longer drags your view
  down while you read history.
